source_metadata.title_raw: Mr. Kevin Padian - A-19-061 - June 11, 2019 - Kensington

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Mr. Kevin Padian 
425 Yale Street 
Kensington, CA 94 708 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-061 
Dear Mr. Padian: 
June 11, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act ("Act") 1 and Government Code Section 1090 et seq.2 Please note that we 
are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and Section 1090, not 
under other conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Public 
Contract Code. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Contra Costa County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Do the conflict of interest provisions of the Act or Section 1090 prohibit you or the 
Kensington Fire Protection Board from participating in decisions regarding the Kensington 
Evacuation Study, which involves contracting with four University of California, Berkeley ("UC 
Berkeley") students and a retired professor while you are employed by UC Berkeley? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Government Code Sections 1090-1097. 7. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. 19-061 
Page No. 2 
No. Under the government income exception, income from your governmental employer is 
not a disqualifying interest under the Act's conflict of interest provisions. In terms of Section 
1090's prohibition, the proposed contract with University students and the University professor 
does not implicate any interest you may have in the University as your employer. Based upon the 
facts provided, you do not have a financial interest in the proposed contract. 
FACTS 
You are on the City of Kensington's Fire Protection Board, a special district. You are also a 
faculty member at UC Berkeley. The Board is undertaking the Kensington Evacuation Study to 
ascertain potential evacuation routes in the City's unincorporated area in case of fire or earthquake. 
In conducting the study, the Fire Protection Board will employ four UC Berkeley graduate 
students and a retired professor. The Board will not contract directly with UC Berkeley. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act: 
The Act only prohibits financial conflicts of interest in governmental decision-making. 
Section 87100 provides that no public official may make, participate in making, or in any way 
att~mpt to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official 
knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. As relevant to your facts, 
Section 87103 defines a "financial interest" as follows: 
( c) Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution 
made in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without 
regard to official status, aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value 
provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to 
the time when the decision is made 
As a Kensington Fire Protection Board Member, you are a public official. Given 
that your employer, UC Berkeley, is implicated in Board contracting to undertake its 
Evacuation Study, you potentially have a conflict of interest predicated on your source of 
income. However, the Act carves out several exceptions to the definition of income. 
(Section 82030(b).) One of the enumerated exceptions is salary received from a state 
government agency. (Section 82030(b )(2).) The University of California system qualifies 
as a state agency for purposes of the Act's conflict of interest provisions. (Section 82049.) 
Accordingly, even if UC Berkeley would be materially affected by Evacuation Study 
decisions, you do not have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act. 
Section 1090: 


File No. 19-061 
Page No. 3 
Section 1090 codifies the common law prohibition against "self-dealing" with respect to 
governmental contracts. Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their 
official capacities, from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition 
applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson 
v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, 
that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering 
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Employees have been 
found to have a financial interest in a contract that involves their employer, even where the contract 
would not result in a change in income or directly involve the employee because an employee has 
an overall interest in the financial success of the firm and continued employment. 
Based upon the facts provided, the Board is not contracting with UC Berkeley, but has a 
proposed contract with four University students and a University professor. Accordingly, the 
contract in question does not implicate any interest you may have in the University as your 
employer. A.s such, you do not have a fin!fficial interest in the contract. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
RPOC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Ryan O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Division
